sentences.text: My name is Dora Nesselroth.
I was born in Poland in 1922 and my father's name is-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Ezra Rozencwajg.
My mother's name was [INAUDIBLE].
They were born also in Skarzysko-Kamienna.
They married in 1917 in Skarzysko-Kamienna.
I have a brother and a sister.
They both are survivors of the Holocaust.
My sister was five years old when a friend of ours came to the ghetto and took my sister and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --and saved her.
They were marvelous people.
My brother went with his father to Buchenwald, but he survived.
My brother's name was Joseph.
My sister's name is Lydia.
She's a medical doctor in New York City.
I grew up in Skarzysko-Kamienna.
I went to school there.
After I finish grammar school, I went to high school.
And I finished high school the same year when the Second World War broke out.
Yeah.
I have a very good life with my parents.
My parents were very loving people, both of them.
They gave-- they tried to give me a good education.
High school at that time, you have to pay tuition.
I went to a private high school because in our town, they haven't that didn't have a government high school.
They have a few in the whole country and the Jews were not permitted to go to a private school at that time.
My father teach me to respect and to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to respect and also to said the truth whatever.
He didn't think that people are bad, they're only people.
Everyone has something good in them and we're supposed to help those people to bring out the good things from a person.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father also-- in Poland, we had-- we-- at that time, the Communist Party, it was not legal but-- to support but the Soviet Union and it didn't success.
My father thought that the Communists-- this was the-- the time when-- in-- in Europe in the '30s, it was like that same thing was in-- in America, that it was a crisis.
People were out of work, they didn't have what to-- how to feed their children.
It was a very terrible time.
So some people who-- the Communist people, the Communist members, would promise you that you're going straight to heaven, you're-- you're going to have everything what your life need.
So they used to go and join the Communist Party.
But this was a lie.
Everything was a lie.
So my father decided that to make a living in Poland, you have to help those people, those poor people-- poor people who don't have a special trade or something.
He opened a night school for the unemployed and for the people who are out of work.
And-- and also, he organized a bank where they don't have to pay-- what to pay-- what do you pay when you go to a bank to-- You mean interest?
Interest, without interest.
He also opened a library.
In Poland, you have to pay to belong to a library at that time.
And-- and those people came to the school.
Their library was in our home.
We had a lot of books.
And-- and he-- and right-- he also organized a-- a theater.
It was not only for the Jews he did it.
He did for all people who would come and go to school.
They would show that they cannot afford to go to school, to pay tuition, something like that, and they were accepted.
And so he had a lot of good friends.
And-- oh, god.
My father has always taught us how to behave, how to be-- how to be good, how to think, how to read, what books to read.
He gave us a suggestion.
He didn't tell us, you have to read it.
But he gave the suggestion, this is good to read.
If you are-- like history, read those books; if you like philosophy, even you don't understand, I come and I will explain to you; and so on.
And make us-- even the youngest were sitting here listening like that, sitting and listening to him.
He was a great father.
My mother-- he also played violin so beautifully that he plays with his soul.
He loved music.
He plays Brahms, he plays-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no.
But musicians, the great composers.
He play-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].

They play-- he plays those beautiful music of Beethoven, he plays music who it's-- that time, in the ghetto especially, at that time it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --you know, it gave me-- I thought at the time that this music what my father used to play at night because we have-- we couldn't-- the Germans didn't allowed us to-- to go out from the houses.
Five o'clock in the-- at night, it was our curfew night and we couldn't go out from the houses.
Everything has to be closed-- the-- the windows, not to see lights in the house, something like that.
So he used to go to a metal room and played there and the music was fantastic.
They took me over from this terrible hell on Earth, ghetto, to a-- the place, I believe that someplace is a better place for to be free.
And that time, I realized that I have to survive, I have to survive because otherwise, I cannot live like that anymore.
And this, I think, made me survive.
But it didn't take too long.

They came over and they're also [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] take away the music instruments, the fur coats, the linen, the silverware, paintings-- everything what they thought that we don't need it anymore.
We didn't know that they were going to do like that.

People came back from-- it was an uprising in Treblinka and people came back to the ghetto, told-- told us that this is the last solution, this is the solution of the Germans, that they will burned us and-- and killed us and to try-- and he want to organize us against the Germans to we uprising there.
But we were sick and-- and hungry.
We didn't believe him that.
My father told him, go away, don't tell those people.
They have to live.
It's impossible.
If-- if the world will know about it, they would not allow to do it.
But we were alone.
Nobody helped us.
Yeah.
My childhood before the war was fantastic.
Of course you go to school, you have to study.
But after school, especially, we went to school six-- six days a week, Saturdays also.
But after that wintertime, I ski and I skate and it was beautiful.
And I came home-- they didn't tell me when I have to go home-- come home.
But the minute I came home, it was 10 o'clock, it was so-- I felt terrible that it's too late to come to the house-- home.
And-- and nobody told me that it's too late or something like that.
They saw my face, my face was my enemy every time.
If I don't like a person, the same thing.
I cannot lie.
This is the whole story of my life.
You see?
And I have a sister, she was a baby.
At that time, she was four years old.
So I took-- I used to take her instead to go for a date, I used to take my sister.
I loved those child like it was my own child.
She was-- she is younger than I am, 14 years younger.
So I used to take her with me to-- she watched me how I ski and on the slides she rolls around.
And I-- I was with her-- I was with her all-- all the time with this child.
And with my brother, same thing.
We came out summertime, we went swimming, we went playing.
We had separate beaches were we can go because beaches were restricted.
We could not go into it, this thing.
But at that time, it didn't bother me so much.
I-- I thought that I'm much happier that-- than they are in there.
At that time even.
As a teenager, I-- I thought that it's impossible how they can live like that with hate.
I-- I-- I couldn't believe it, that hate is so very important to them.
The-- the boys from-- Christian boys.
Not all of them.
We cannot condemn everyone.
You see, I have Mr.
[INAUDIBLE], who saved my sister, Lydia.
And he was of-- I don't know, an angel.
He came to the ghetto, he came into the ghetto, take out the sister, and helped her.
14 years younger.
Yeah.
Yeah.
They used to throw stones, they used to throw those-- wintertime there are snowballs.
And they were very [INAUDIBLE].
Sometimes I have a-- a bleeding nose coming home.
But I never cry.
I never cry.
Because I-- always my father would say, yeah, those children from those families who are not educated enough, who don't know how to raise children.
The same children are going to hate their parents, you know?
And so I always thought about that.
He never said, you have to hate them back or you have to revenge.
Because to this day, if I hate somebody, I hate myself first.
Of course.
I have a lot of friends.
We always go out together.
We went to the movies together, we read books together, we review books together.
When children used to go together in school with me.
You saw the big picture there that the two of them were in the school, together with me in school.
And we did together everything that's possible.
Traditionally.
My father was not a very-- he finish yeshiva because the father, my grandfather, didn't allow for him to go to a-- a public school because they don't speak-- they have to speak Polish.
Because Hebrews for them-- he was an orthodox Jew.
And my father used to keep books and adding to learn and to-- he finished high school [INAUDIBLE] himself.
He went to school and he had his diploma, you know.
And then he went to the university.
Oh, we-- we went-- summertime went to-- to the forest in the morning for a trek for mushrooms.
It was the nicest time.
We went to the forest to have raspberries, you know, while the smell is so good, so, so good.
And five o'clock in the morning.
I used to go with my father-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --to drive a bicycle for about four, five kilometers.
And this was the nicest thing.
We drove together.
And one day coming back, we stopped in the village to a peasant and he bought those fresh [INAUDIBLE] they call it, buttermilk is here.
And-- and eggs-- I don't want-- I didn't want to eat eggs because I saw how the eggs came from.
I came to this thing.
I came home and I said, no, I'm not going to eat these eggs, you know.
But you see, everything is such-- now, you think about it, how different.
It was very different than-- than-- than now.
You know, everything was different.
It was a beautiful time of my life.
I'm nostalgic maybe, too.
Because you get older, you think a little deeper and more nostalgic, especially when you're sick and you see that you didn't accomplish what you want to accomplished, you see.
And I accomplish a lot in this United States.
I was so grateful that they accepted me as a-- a citizen of the United States.
And when-- wherever I work here, I never asked for a raise because I-- I was so grateful that they let me work.
They accepted me.
This was the nicest thing.
And another thing, also.
When I saw a policeman, it reminds me of other policeman and in the beginning I was scared of them.
I always thought that this is the-- the kind of police that we have out of Poland.
You know?
Let's go back to your school days.
Can you describe to us what high school or elementary was like?
Yeah.
High school was a private high school.
The name of the high school was [POLISH].
He came back from Lithuania and he-- he built a big, big building with 2,000 students.
And they have different faculties there.
They have faculties like trade schools, they have technical schools, and they have high schools-- gymnasium, they call it.
No.
When-- when I went to school the school was very young at that time.
And we have to study very hard, of course.
And especially when you were Jewish, you studied much harder than others do.
At the end of the year, we called professor our school because everyone has a doctor degree in school, in high school.
And he would say, I have to say something about Dora Rozencwajg.
She is-- he couldn't say straight, that I am a good student or something like that.
Yeah.
Not all of the teachers.
He said that I-- I want to say something not because Rozencwajg or something like that.
And-- I-- he stopped talking about it.
He gave me the report card and that's it.
I have a teacher, English teacher, and I was 14 years old.
And I have read the book, yes?
In Polish it's [POLISH].
Very-- very hard work for a 14-year child to comprehend it, but I-- I did.
And we have to read six books in school.
And the other six, whatever you want to read.
So one day, I-- she came in the morning and she said, [INAUDIBLE] to read something.
I said, I have read this book.
And she want to interview with me then.
The director of school came in to interview, to listen.
And we discussed this everything and again she tried this and that.
And I asked her an answer.
And he asked her what grade I have for Polish literature, Polish everything.
And she said-- I said-- I didn't et her finish.
I said, I have a B because I'm Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he looked at me and I start crying the first time.
And he said to me, came to my office after-- after the hour is finished.
So I came to the office and he said to me, remember, if you are going to-- to study and be so good like I heard you today, I am not going to let you have a lower-- a lower grade.
So every time when I came home and I told about this teacher to my father, my father used to say, you go to school not for the grades but for knowledge.
Remember that.
Never-- and I think that he went to school and intervened with this professor, [POLISH].
Have I forget my name, I forget, you know, and I forget other things, but her I cannot forget.
Do you know when I lived in New York, I didn't speak English because I was afraid to open my mouth because I make mistake.
And nobody-- the people here so polite that nobody would correct me.
And I begged everyone because this became a little like a hobby.
If you say something wrong, you will correct-- you will always repeat yourself.
Here, in the United States.
And I was scared because you have to be perfect, like I am perfect in Polish-- perfect.
Because of her.
She was a very good teacher.
But she had her own opinion.
Another opinion, it was what do you call those people who go to school and they have to learn to rad everything in the United States?
Yeah.
In the United States, they go to-- to universities and they took those-- no.
Oh, God.
What do they call it?
Forget it.
I cannot remember.
